scope: v0.15 km-bench: GPU step-throughput + peak-VRAM vs column count

base_grid.note: the REAL d02 case is 66x159x44 = 10,494 cols, NOT 128x128; the 128x128/16384-col baseline in the brief does not match this fixture. Tile factors are relative to 66x159.

config_notes.note: Larger grids are COST proxies built by spatially tiling the real 128x128 state/grid; physics kernel cost depends on array shapes (ncol,nz) not values. boundary/GWD/NoahMP disabled uniformly at ALL sizes so the scaling law is apples-to-apples on the dycore+radiation+PBL core. This core 128x128 ms/step is BELOW the full production ~178 ms/step that also pays boundary+GWD+NoahMP.

warmed_method: two-hours-point marginal: per_step=(warm_h2-warm_h1)/(n2-n1), removes fixed per-call exec overhead; warm_h2 = mean of two samples; donate-safe re-tiled state per timed call.

records.error: single 28.44 GiB allocation > free; MaxAllocSize 19.08 GiB, device 33.66 GB total

largest_grid_that_fit.note: largest grid that ran in ~30 GiB; next size up (209,880 cols) OOMs on a 28.44 GiB single op

saturation_verdict.summary: ms/step is ~LINEAR in column count (R2=0.997, slope ~4.9-5.0 ms/1000col). The MARGINAL per-column slope RISES from 4.1 to 5.5 ms/1000col as the grid grows (it does not fall), and per-column throughput PLATEAUS at ~195-200 cols/ms and slightly DECLINES at the top (194 at 168k). A launch-bound, GPU-idle step would show ms/step nearly CONSTANT with ncol and throughput RISING steeply; neither is observed. Bigger grids therefore do NOT saturate the GPU better here -- they cost proportionally more wall time. The mild overall sublinearity (ncol x16 -> ms/step x13.9, ratio 0.87) is entirely the ~20 ms fixed per-step intercept (per-call dispatch + the 16,384-column radiation/MYNN tile floor), NOT genuine per-column saturation headroom.

oom_ceiling.note: Even with cuda_async, 209,880 cols needs one 28.44 GiB op (and a 19.08 GiB resident buffer) -> exceeds the 33.66 GB device once other live buffers are held. A single op that large means some radiation/PBL intermediate is NOT bounded by the 16,384-column tile floor and scales with full ncol; that un-chunked buffer is the practical VRAM ceiling, well before compute would.

raw_runs_note: 4 smallest points measured under default BFC allocator (faithful low peak_bytes_in_use); 3 largest under XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_ALLOCATOR=cuda_async to bypass BFC fragmentation (BFC OOMed on one ~9.5 GiB op at 94k cols despite only 9 GiB peak). ms/step is allocator-independent (compute identical) and pooled; VRAM peaks are tagged by allocator. async peaks are the headline since they reached the largest grids. Per-allocator raw artifacts: bench run = this file's prior version; async run = largest_probe.json.
